title: Corned Beef Hash
subtitle: Tinned corned beef fried with potato and onion until the edges are crisp and savoury
excerpt: Corned beef hash is a storecupboard classic: tinned corned beef broken into a pan with cooked potato and onion and fried until the edges crisp and the bottom develops a golden crust. It is ready in 20 minutes and takes no skill to get right.
sourceNotes: Traditional British storecupboard cooking. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: beef hash from cold meat. American influence on British tinned-meat cooking from the twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pre-boil the potatoes and refrigerate. Cold potato crisps better in the pan.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beef cured in a salt brine (the 'corn' refers to the large crystals of salt, called corns, used in the curing). Tinned corned beef is a pressed, cooked product sold in the distinctive trapezoid can. It has a soft, dense texture and a strongly savoury flavour.

The browned, crisp layer that forms on the base of a hash when it is left undisturbed in a hot pan. It is the most prized part; scraping it up and folding it through the softer upper layer is the defining technique of a well-made hash.

content.text: is one of the most reliable storecupboard ingredients in British cooking. It needs nothing more than cold potato, onion, and a hot pan. The skill in hash is patience: leave it alone long enough to form the

items.prepNote: use 2 x 340 g tins of corned beef, roughly broken into pieces
medium, boiled in their skins, cooled, peeled, and roughly crushed

content.text: in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the
and cook for 8 minutes until golden. Add the crushed potato, season with
and press flat. Cook without disturbing for 5 minutes.
Add the corned beef and crisp

Break the corned beef into large chunks and scatter over the potato. Add the Worcestershire sauce if using. Stir everything together roughly, then press flat again. Cook for 5 minutes until the base develops a deep brown crust. Turn in sections, scraping up the crust, and cook for another 5 minutes to crisp the other side.

rows.symptom: Hash is soft and grey with no crust
rows.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too much liquid in the potato
rows.fix: Use a large pan over high heat. Ensure the potato is fully cold and dry before it goes in.

rows.cause: Broken into pieces too small, or stirred too often
rows.fix: Leave the corned beef in larger pieces and stir only once, to turn the crust.

rows.cause: Corned beef is already highly salted; no extra salt is needed
rows.fix: Do not salt the hash during cooking. Serve with something acidic such as ketchup or pickled cabbage to balance.

rows.symptom: Bottom is burnt before the top is hot through
rows.fix: Reduce to medium heat after the initial browning.

content.text: Corned beef hash entered British home cooking via American military rations and tinned food culture of the mid-twentieth century. It became a staple of the British weekly meal plan as an economical hot supper, and remains a comfort food associated with frugal simplicity. Serve with ketchup and pickled onions.
